stories.title: India's biggest earnings day of the season: nearly 60 companies report results, from Adani Power to Nestle to Zomato's parent
key_points: Close to 60 companies report April-June (Q1 FY27) results on the same day
Big names include Adani Power, Adani Green, Nestle India, Dr Reddy's, BPCL, HPCL, and Eternal (Zomato's parent)
Results land the same week as a three-day losing streak for the Sensex and Nifty, driven by high oil prices
Investors will use these results to judge how much the Iran-war oil spike and new US tariffs are already denting Indian corporate profits
stories.background: Indian listed companies report earnings once a quarter, and in a fast-growing economy like India's, these results are one of the clearest windows into how businesses are actually holding up versus how the stock market is pricing them. This particular quarter (April-June, called Q1 of the FY27 fiscal year that started in April) is being watched more closely than usual because it lands right as oil prices are spiking due to the Iran war and Indian exporters are getting hit with new US tariffs — both of which raise costs or squeeze revenue for many of the companies reporting.
stories.what_happened: Nearly 60 companies released April-June quarter results on Wednesday, one of the busiest single earnings days of the season. Reporting companies span energy (Adani Power, Adani Green Energy, BPCL, HPCL), consumer goods (Nestle India, United Spirits), pharmaceuticals (Dr Reddy's Laboratories), and consumer internet (Eternal, the parent company of food-delivery app Zomato), among dozens of others including SRF, NTPC Green, and Schaeffler India.
stories.why_it_matters: With this many large, differently-exposed companies reporting on the same day, the results collectively work like a stress test for the Indian economy right now — showing analysts and investors how much of the recent damage from high oil prices (which hits fuel-heavy businesses and raises companies' costs) and new US tariffs (which hits exporters like pharma) is actually showing up in company profits, versus how much is still just market anxiety.
stories.ripple_effects: Strong results from oil-refining and marketing companies like BPCL and HPCL could ease some investor worry about margin pressure from expensive crude, while weak results from export-facing companies like Dr Reddy's could confirm fears about the US generic-drug tariff. Because so many sectors report together, the combined reaction can move the Sensex and Nifty indices more than any single company's results normally would, feeding into the broader three-day slide those indices are already in.
stories.why_now: This is a scheduled quarterly reporting date, but its market impact is amplified by timing: it falls in the same week as the Iran-driven oil price spike, the looming India-US trade deadline, and a rupee near record lows — all pressures these earnings will help investors size up.
stories.watch_next: Whether upcoming results from other large-cap names this week confirm a broader earnings slowdown or show Indian companies absorbing the oil and tariff shocks better than feared.

key_terms.definition: The first quarter (April-June 2026) of India's 2026-27 fiscal year, which for Indian companies and the government runs April to March rather than January to December.

stories.the_lesson: When many companies across different industries report earnings in the same window, markets treat it less like individual news and more like a single readout on the health of the whole economy — that's why 'earnings season' can move stock indices even when no single company surprises anyone.
stories.market_link: Directly moves Nifty and Sensex given the number and size of companies reporting; watch oil-marketing companies (BPCL, HPCL) and pharma (Dr Reddy's) for read-through on the two biggest pressures on Indian corporates right now — oil prices and US tariffs.

stories.title: India-US trade deal is stuck at the 'last 1%' of legal text with just two days left before tariffs jump
key_points: A temporary US tariff arrangement on Indian goods expires July 24 — two days from now
The deal is reportedly down to final legal wording, but nothing is signed yet
Earlier in 2026, the US already cut its reciprocal tariff on India from 50% to 18%
India is holding out for better terms than Asian rivals rather than rushing to beat the deadline
stories.background: Trade deals between countries usually take years, but the US and India have been negotiating an interim ('first-phase') bilateral trade agreement at unusual speed this year under pressure from President Trump's tariff policy. Earlier in 2026, Washington agreed to lower its 'reciprocal tariff' — a tax the US charges on imports roughly matching what it says the other country charges American exports — on Indian goods from 50% to 18%, partly in exchange for India agreeing to stop buying Russian oil and to purchase more American goods. But that lower rate, and a temporary 10% global tariff Trump also imposed on all trading partners for 150 days, is set to expire, which is what makes the July 24 deadline significant.
stories.what_happened: As of this week, negotiators describe the India-US Bilateral Trade Agreement as parked at the 'last 1 per cent' of legal text — meaning the substance is largely agreed but nothing has actually been signed or published. The interim US tariff arrangement, including the extra 10% reciprocal levy on all countries, is due to lapse on July 24. A senior US official has said the two sides are 'very, very close' to a deal, but India's negotiators have said publicly they are prioritizing getting favorable terms — particularly tariff parity with competing Asian exporters like Vietnam — over rushing to beat the calendar deadline.
stories.why_it_matters: If the deadline passes without a signed deal, Indian exporters could see tariffs snap back toward higher default rates just as they're already absorbing a separate steep US tariff on generic drugs, one of India's largest export categories. A finalized deal, by contrast, would lock in the lower 18% rate and open India's market wider to US farm and industrial goods — a trade-off that affects everything from Indian pharma company earnings to US soybean farmers.
stories.ripple_effects: Indian exporters in pharmaceuticals, textiles, and manufacturing face tariff uncertainty right up to the deadline, which can delay shipping and investment decisions. A deal would commit India to cutting its own tariffs on US industrial goods and farm products (from tree nuts to wine), potentially raising competition for Indian producers in those categories. Missing the deadline without an extension could reignite the tariff tensions that have already weighed on Indian markets this month alongside the oil-price shock from the Iran war.
stories.why_now: The clock is the story: the current tariff arrangement's 150-day window runs out on July 24, forcing both sides to either finalize an agreement, extend the interim terms, or let tariffs reset higher within the next two days.
stories.watch_next: Whether India and the US sign a deal, announce an extension, or let the July 24 deadline pass without either.

key_terms.definition: A tariff the US sets on imports from a country roughly matching what that country is judged to charge on US exports.

stories.the_lesson: Trade negotiations often look nearly finished for a long time — being down to the 'last 1%' of legal text can still mean weeks of delay, because the final details are usually the most politically sensitive ones.
stories.market_link: A signed deal would be a relief for India-exposed pharma, textile, and IT-services stocks; a missed deadline without an extension would add to the pressure already on the Sensex and Nifty from high oil prices.

stories.title: Lok Sabha takes up a bill to expand the Supreme Court from 33 to 37 judges, replacing May's ordinance
key_points: Bill raises the Supreme Court's sanctioned strength from 33 to 37 judges (excluding the CJI)
It formally replaces an ordinance the government issued back in May
Law Minister Arjun Ram Meghwal is steering it through the Lok Sabha this week
Aimed at cutting the court's long-standing case backlog
stories.background: The number of judges allowed on India's Supreme Court isn't fixed by the Constitution — Parliament sets it by law, and has raised it several times before as the court's caseload grew. In May 2026, the government used an ordinance (a temporary law the President can issue when Parliament isn't in session) to raise the court's strength from 33 to 37 judges. Under India's Constitution, an ordinance automatically lapses a set number of weeks after Parliament reconvenes unless lawmakers pass it as a regular Act — which is exactly what this bill now does.
stories.what_happened: Union Law Minister Arjun Ram Meghwal introduced the Supreme Court (Number of Judges) Amendment Bill, 2026 in the Lok Sabha, formally converting May's ordinance into permanent law and raising the Supreme Court's sanctioned judge strength from 33 to 37 (a figure that excludes the Chief Justice of India). The Lok Sabha is taking it up for consideration and passage this week during the ongoing Monsoon Session.
stories.why_it_matters: India's Supreme Court has a well-documented backlog of pending cases that can leave litigants waiting years for a hearing. Four more judges won't clear that backlog alone, but it's a concrete lever the government can pull to increase the court's hearing capacity.
stories.ripple_effects: More judges means more benches can sit simultaneously, potentially speeding up both routine hearings and larger constitution-bench cases. It also puts pressure on the Collegium — the panel of senior judges that recommends who gets appointed — to identify and clear four qualified names relatively quickly.
stories.why_now: This bill is on the floor now specifically because of the constitutional clock: an ordinance lapses if Parliament doesn't convert it into law within a fixed window after reconvening, so this had to come up during the current Monsoon Session regardless of the political noise around NEET protests.
stories.watch_next: Whether the bill clears the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha without amendment this session, and how quickly the Collegium sends up names to fill the four new seats.

key_terms.definition: A law the President can issue when Parliament isn't in session, which has the same force as an Act but must be approved by Parliament within a set time or it lapses.
The system where India's senior-most judges, not the government, recommend who becomes a judge — a process unique to the Indian judiciary.

stories.the_lesson: Executive ordinances in India are a stopgap, not a shortcut — any change made while Parliament is out of session has to survive a return trip through the legislature, which is exactly the check now playing out with this bill.

stories.title: Modi launches ₹5,650 crore worth of energy and infrastructure projects in Tamil Nadu's Tiruchirappalli
key_points: Modi inaugurated/launched projects worth about ₹5,650 crore in Trichy
Biggest piece: a ₹3,700 crore gas pipeline network for Nilgiris and Erode districts
Also flagged off 5 new trains and 370 km of rural roads
Visit doubled as an NDA political rally in a state where BJP is historically weak
stories.background: Tamil Nadu is run by the DMK, a party that has long kept the BJP at arm's length, making it a state where the central government's infrastructure spending carries an extra political charge ahead of state elections. Modi's government has repeatedly used project launches like this one to build a development narrative in states it doesn't govern.
stories.what_happened: Modi inaugurated and laid foundation stones for around ₹5,650 crore (about $680 million) in projects across Tamil Nadu. The centerpiece is a ₹3,700 crore City Gas Distribution network for the Nilgiris and Erode districts, which will pipe cooking and industrial gas to nearly 9 lakh (900,000) households and businesses. He also dedicated an Indian Oil lube-blending plant in Chennai to the nation, flagged off five new train services including two Amrit Bharat Express trains, inaugurated 370 km of rural roads under the PM Gram Sadak Yojana, and addressed an NDA political rally.
stories.why_it_matters: This is the central government spending real money — and political capital — in a state it doesn't control, timed to coincide with a tense Parliament session back in Delhi. The gas pipeline alone will change how nearly a million households and businesses get their fuel.
stories.ripple_effects: State-run oil companies BPCL and IOC carry out these projects, so their capex and order books are directly affected. The visit also sets up a familiar tussle: the Centre claims credit for development, while the state government argues its own contribution is being overlooked — a recurring dynamic in Centre-versus-opposition-ruled-state politics.
stories.why_now: The timing lines up with Parliament's contentious Monsoon Session in Delhi, where the NEET paper-leak protests have dominated headlines — giving Modi a chance to project a development story from outside the capital while that fight continues.
stories.watch_next: How DMK and other Tamil Nadu opposition parties respond, and whether the announced projects hit their construction timelines.

key_terms.definition: A network of pipelines that delivers natural gas directly to homes and businesses for cooking, heating or vehicle fuel, instead of using cylinders or trucked fuel.
A federal scheme that funds all-weather roads connecting rural villages to the wider road network.

stories.the_lesson: Infrastructure launches by a national government in a state it doesn't politically control usually serve two purposes at once — genuine development and political messaging — so it pays to read the economics and the politics of such visits together.
stories.market_link: BPCL and IOC (both listed PSUs) are the executing companies for the gas and fuel projects — watch for capex guidance updates from either.

stories.title: Supreme Court refuses to block 6 Shiv Sena (UBT) MPs' merger into Shinde's party — but agrees to hear the challenge
key_points: Shiv Sena (UBT) asked the Supreme Court to block the Speaker's recognition of 6 of its Lok Sabha MPs merging into Eknath Shinde's rival Shiv Sena
The Court declined to stay the Speaker's decision but issued notice, with the next hearing in two weeks
The merger cut Shiv Sena (UBT)'s Lok Sabha strength to just 3 MPs
It's the same anti-defection-law fight now playing out for TMC too, this time reaching the Lok Sabha instead of a state assembly
stories.background: The Shiv Sena split in 2022, when Eknath Shinde broke away from Uddhav Thackeray's Shiv Sena with a large group of legislators; the Election Commission later recognized Shinde's faction as the 'real' Shiv Sena, keeping the party's name and bow-and-arrow symbol, while Thackeray's remnant became known as Shiv Sena (UBT). The two sides have been locked in overlapping legal and political battles ever since, mostly over state assembly seats — this new fight is about Lok Sabha MPs instead.
stories.what_happened: On 18 July, Lok Sabha Speaker Om Birla approved the merger of six Shiv Sena (UBT) MPs — Sanjay Deshmukh, Sanjay Bandu Jadhav, Sanjay Dina Patil, Nagesh Patil-Ashtikar, Omprakash Rajenimbalkar and Bhausaheb Wakchaure — into Eknath Shinde's Shiv Sena. UBT, through leader Arvind Sawant, challenged that decision in the Supreme Court, arguing the six were elected on the UBT ticket and that the Speaker had no basis to recognize a 'merger' of just six individuals into a rival party. A bench of Justices P.S. Narasimha and Alok Aradhe declined to pause the Speaker's decision but issued formal notice to the Speaker's office and the six MPs, directing them to respond, with the case returning in about two weeks.
stories.why_it_matters: This tests, at the national level, the same anti-defection-law question already reshaping Maharashtra's state politics: can a Speaker effectively legalize a group of defections by simply calling it a 'merger'? A loss for UBT would shrink Uddhav Thackeray's national footprint to just 3 Lok Sabha MPs and set a precedent other parties — and Speakers — could point to elsewhere.
stories.ripple_effects: A win for Shinde's side strengthens the BJP-led alliance both in Maharashtra and in Delhi, weakens UBT's leverage inside the opposition INDIA bloc, and keeps a legal precedent alive that could be cited in other, similar disputes playing out this same week — including the ongoing TMC-versus-NCPI fight in the Lok Sabha.
stories.why_now: UBT moved court within days of the Speaker's 18 July approval, right as the Monsoon Session began, and the Supreme Court heard the plea within 24 hours — making this fast-moving, live litigation rather than old news.
stories.watch_next: The counter-affidavits due from the Speaker's office and the six MPs, and the next hearing in roughly two weeks, which could determine whether the Court ultimately upholds or overturns the Speaker's merger recognition.

key_terms.definition: A constitutional rule that normally disqualifies a lawmaker who switches parties, with an exception if enough of the original party's members merge together at once.
A court order pausing an action while it considers the full case — the Court here declined to grant one, meaning the merger stands for now.
The ruling alliance in Maharashtra state made up of Eknath Shinde's Shiv Sena, the BJP, and a faction of the NCP.

stories.the_lesson: Anti-defection law was written to stop individual lawmakers from switching sides for personal gain, but its 'merger' exception can become a loophole when enough members move together — the same legal mechanism keeps resurfacing across different parties and states because the underlying incentive never goes away.

stories.title: TMC's Kalyan Banerjee suspended from the Lok Sabha after a clash with MPs who defected to a new party
key_points: Trinamool Congress MP Kalyan Banerjee was suspended from the Lok Sabha for the rest of the Monsoon Session
He clashed with MPs from NCPI — a new party formed by 20 former TMC MPs who defected after Mamata Banerjee's crushing state-election defeat
The Speaker acted on a written complaint from NCPI MPs over remarks made during the altercation
It's the first floor clash between TMC's loyalist rump and the breakaway bloc since the split
stories.background: In May 2026, the BJP won a landslide 206 of 293 seats in the West Bengal state election, ending Mamata Banerjee's 15-year rule as chief minister. The shock triggered a rebellion inside her own party's Lok Sabha benches in Delhi: 20 of TMC's 28 MPs broke away and merged into NCPI (Nationalist Citizens Party of India), a smaller party, in a move designed to clear the two-thirds-of-members threshold that lets lawmakers switch parties without being disqualified under India's anti-defection law. That merger made NCPI the Lok Sabha's fifth-largest party and a new ally of the BJP-led government, while just 8 MPs — including Kalyan Banerjee — stayed loyal to Mamata and Abhishek Banerjee.
stories.what_happened: On Wednesday, Banerjee got into a heated verbal exchange with NCPI MPs — his former TMC colleagues — shortly after the House adjourned, reportedly involving objectionable remarks directed at a woman NCPI MP. NCPI MPs complained in writing to Speaker Om Birla, who directed the government to act; a resolution to suspend Banerjee for the rest of the session was moved and passed by voice vote, and he was ordered to leave the Parliament premises immediately.
stories.why_it_matters: This isn't just an MP losing his temper — it's the TMC's internal civil war spilling onto the floor of Parliament for the first time since the defection, showing how personal and bitter the split has become and testing what authority Mamata and Abhishek Banerjee have left in Delhi after losing both the state government and two-thirds of their Lok Sabha bench.
stories.ripple_effects: The episode likely pushes NCPI closer to the BJP-led government that now counts it as an ally, raises questions about whether more of TMC's remaining 8 MPs could eventually defect too, and is a fresh blow to the opposition INDIA bloc's unity as one of its founding parties visibly fractures on camera.
stories.why_now: The Speaker only formally approved the 20 MPs' merger into NCPI on 18 July, just before this Monsoon Session began — meaning this was the very first week the two rival camps have shared the Lok Sabha chamber as separate parties, all but guaranteeing friction.
stories.watch_next: Whether Banerjee apologizes or challenges the suspension, whether TMC escalates the standoff, and whether any more of the party's remaining 8 Lok Sabha MPs follow the earlier defectors to NCPI.

key_terms.definition: A constitutional rule (the Tenth Schedule) that normally disqualifies a lawmaker who switches parties — unless enough of the original party's members merge together at once.
One of Parliament's three yearly sittings, held around India's monsoon months (typically July-August).
A vote decided by how loudly members shout 'aye' or 'no', rather than a formal headcount.

stories.the_lesson: When a party loses power, the fallout often isn't just electoral — it can fracture the party itself, as members calculate their own political survival is better served elsewhere. Watching where a defeated party's own lawmakers go next often reveals more about the state of play than the election result itself.

stories.title: Sensex and Nifty extend losses to a third day as Brent crude tops $92 on Middle East fears
key_points: Sensex fell over 400 points and Nifty slipped below 24,100 in early trade Wednesday — a third straight losing session
Brent crude climbed to around $92 a barrel, a five-week high, on fears the Iran-Gulf conflict could disrupt oil supply
Bank stocks led the fall, alongside caution ahead of key corporate earnings this week
Comes on top of Indian pharma stocks already reeling from Trump's new generic-drug tariff
stories.background: India imports roughly 85% of the crude oil it consumes, so global oil-price swings pass through quickly to Indian markets, the rupee, and inflation. Markets have already been under pressure this week from the escalating US-Iran war pushing oil higher, the RBI's emergency dollar-inflow scheme, and a new US tariff on generic drug exports that hit pharma stocks.
stories.what_happened: The BSE Sensex declined around 400-700 points and the NSE Nifty slipped below 24,100 in Wednesday trade, marking a third consecutive day of losses. The drop was driven by Brent crude rising to roughly $92 a barrel — its highest in five weeks — as Iran's renewed strikes on Gulf states raised fresh fears of supply disruption. Banking stocks and other index heavyweights led the decline.
stories.why_it_matters: Sustained market falls erode retail investor wealth and signal rising caution among institutional investors. Because oil is a major import for India, this shows how a war thousands of miles away shows up almost immediately in Indian stock prices, the rupee, and household fuel costs.
stories.ripple_effects: Further rupee weakness could prompt more RBI intervention, and higher crude costs typically pass through to pump prices and inflation within weeks, squeezing consumers and companies alike.
stories.why_now: Today's fall was driven directly by Iran's fresh strikes on US allies in the Gulf (see the linked story), which pushed oil prices to a five-week high overnight.
stories.watch_next: Whether Brent crude crosses $95 a barrel, the RBI's next dollar-supply intervention, and this week's Q1 bank earnings.

key_terms.definition: The international benchmark price for oil that most of the world's crude, including what India imports, is priced against.

stories.the_lesson: Because India imports most of its oil, geopolitical shocks on the other side of the world show up directly in Indian stock and currency markets within hours — a reminder that 'local' markets are rarely insulated from global events.
stories.market_link: Direct read for Nifty/Sensex, oil-marketing companies (higher input costs), and the rupee; IT and pharma exporters may see mixed effects from a weaker rupee offsetting tariff pain.

thread.previously: Sensex and Nifty fell for a second straight day yesterday as the US-Iran war pushed crude oil toward $90 a barrel.
thread.changed: The losing streak extended to a third day today as Brent crude broke above $92 — a five-week high — after Iran's fresh strikes on Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan, with bank stocks now leading the decline.

stories.title: NEET protests escalate: opposition wears black in Parliament, CJP says 'no more talks' as Wangchuk's hunger strike hits day 24
key_points: Opposition MPs led by Rahul Gandhi and Priyanka Gandhi Vadra wore black in Parliament today, a day after their sit-in at PM Modi's residence
The Cockroach Janata Party (CJP), which is running the Jantar Mantar protest, says it will no longer negotiate with the government
Union ministers JP Nadda and Jitendra Singh visited hunger-striker Sonam Wangchuk in hospital as his fast enters its 24th day
Lok Sabha was repeatedly disrupted as the opposition pressed for Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation
stories.background: NEET is India's national medical-entrance exam. A leaked question paper triggered weeks of student protests and a hunger strike by activist Sonam Wangchuk, demanding accountability and Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation. Police action against protesting students in Delhi further inflamed the standoff, and on July 21 Rahul Gandhi and Priyanka Gandhi Vadra were detained after marching on the Prime Minister's residence — the first time a Leader of the Opposition had protested there.
stories.what_happened: Today, opposition MPs including Rahul Gandhi, Priyanka Gandhi Vadra and Samajwadi Party's Akhilesh Yadav wore black clothes on Parliament's premises in solidarity with the protesting students, as the Lok Sabha saw repeated adjournments over the issue. The CJP's founder said there would be no further talks with the government. Separately, two Union ministers visited Wangchuk, now 24 days into his hunger strike, at a Gurugram hospital.
stories.why_it_matters: The crisis has moved from a student issue into a full-blown political standoff testing the government's handling of unrest and the credibility of the national medical entrance exam. The opposition's escalating, unified protests — including an unprecedented demonstration by the Leader of the Opposition near the PM's residence — signal it sees political opportunity in the government's handling of the episode.
stories.ripple_effects: Continued disruption could stall other business in Parliament's ongoing Monsoon Session, delay any resolution for NEET-affected students' academic year, and increase pressure on Dharmendra Pradhan either to resign or for the government to order a re-examination.
stories.why_now: The stand-off hardened after yesterday's detentions of Rahul and Priyanka Gandhi, prompting the CJP to harden its position and the opposition to escalate with a coordinated Parliament protest today.
stories.watch_next: Whether the government orders a re-exam, whether Pradhan resigns, and the next health update on Wangchuk's hunger strike.

key_terms.definition: National Eligibility cum Entrance Test — the single national exam Indian students must clear to get into medical college.

stories.the_lesson: When one side declares it will no longer negotiate, a standoff usually escalates rather than resolves — the other side is then forced to choose between a costly concession or a prolonged, more damaging crisis.

thread.previously: Rahul Gandhi and Priyanka Gandhi Vadra were detained by Delhi Police yesterday after marching on PM Modi's residence to protest the NEET paper leak and the police crackdown on student protesters.
thread.changed: The opposition escalated further today with a coordinated black-clothes protest inside Parliament, the protest group leading the Jantar Mantar sit-in ruled out further talks, and two Union ministers visited hunger-striker Sonam Wangchuk in hospital as his fast passed the three-week mark.

stories.title: Assam's flood toll nearly triples in a day to 31 dead, as the Brahmaputra breaches danger levels in 12 districts
key_points: 21 more people died in Assam in the 24 hours to Tuesday — this year's toll now 31
5.64 lakh (564,000) people affected across 12 districts
The Brahmaputra and several tributaries are now above their danger marks
13 of the new deaths were in Sivasagar district alone, including four women and two children
stories.background: Assam sits on the floodplain of the Brahmaputra, one of the world's largest rivers, and floods every monsoon (India's June-September rainy season) to some degree. This year's monsoon has been unusually intense in the northeast: flash floods and landslides had already killed dozens across Assam, Kashmir and Nagaland over the past few days, with the state's disaster management authority reporting a toll past 10 as of Monday.
stories.what_happened: The Assam State Disaster Management Authority said the flood situation worsened sharply on Wednesday: 21 people died in the preceding 24 hours alone, taking the state's 2026 monsoon death toll to 31, with more than 5.64 lakh people affected across 12 districts. Sivasagar district recorded 13 of the new deaths, including four women and two children; Charaideo recorded five, Golaghat two, and Jorhat one. The Brahmaputra is flowing above its danger mark at Neamatighat, and tributaries including the Buridihing and Dhansiri rivers are also above danger levels. Authorities are running 273 relief camps housing over 12,000 displaced people, and the India Meteorological Department has forecast more rain in the coming days.
stories.why_it_matters: A death toll roughly tripling in a single day, with over half a million people affected, marks a serious escalation of what was already a bad flood season — and forecasters say more rain, not less, is coming. This is now one of the more severe flood emergencies Assam has faced in recent years, straining relief infrastructure that is already running hundreds of camps.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect disruption to Assam's tea, agriculture and transport sectors, pressure on the state and central governments to speed up relief funds, and renewed debate over embankment maintenance and flood preparedness. Prolonged flooding on this scale also typically triggers waterborne disease risk in relief camps in the following weeks.
stories.why_now: Incessant rain through the week pushed river levels past danger marks across multiple districts simultaneously, causing the death toll and displacement numbers to jump within a single 24-hour reporting cycle.
stories.watch_next: Whether the Brahmaputra keeps rising as more rain arrives, and whether the death toll and displacement figures keep climbing through the rest of the week.

key_terms.definition: A river-level threshold set by India's Central Water Commission; once a river crosses it, flooding of nearby low-lying areas is considered imminent or already underway.

stories.the_lesson: Flood tolls can escalate non-linearly — a river sitting just below its danger mark can look manageable for days, then cross a threshold and multiply casualties and displacement within 24 hours, which is why disaster response has to be pre-positioned rather than reactive.

thread.previously: We reported Monday that Assam's flood toll had passed 10 with over 3 lakh people affected, alongside separate deadly flooding in Kashmir's Poonch, Rajouri and Doda districts.
thread.changed: The Assam toll alone has now nearly tripled to 31 (21 dead in a single day), with affected people up to 5.64 lakh and the Brahmaputra now above its danger mark in multiple districts — this is a serious escalation, not a continuation of the same level of flooding.

stories.title: RBI's emergency dollar-inflow scheme pulls in $20.72 billion in six weeks — more than expected
key_points: RBI's swap/deposit scheme has raised $20.72bn since June 8, mostly via NRI bank deposits
That's well ahead of pace — analysts now expect $60-70bn in total inflows
The scheme exists to help defend the rupee, which has been sliding as the Iran-Israel war pushes oil prices up
It's a sign the RBI has a real lever to pull even as global oil-driven pressure builds
stories.background: India imports most of its oil, so when crude prices rise — as they have with the ongoing US-Iran conflict — India has to spend more dollars buying it, and the rupee tends to weaken. To fight that, the Reserve Bank of India (RBI, India's central bank) launched a package on June 5 offering banks a concessional (subsidized) 'swap' deal: banks get a better exchange rate if they bring in fresh foreign-currency deposits from Non-Resident Indians (NRIs) or foreign loans, encouraging dollars to flow into India specifically to prop up the currency and the country's foreign exchange (forex) reserves — the RBI's war chest for defending the rupee.
stories.what_happened: The RBI said Tuesday its measures mobilised $20.72 billion between June 8 and July 17: $17.40bn through FCNR(B) deposits (foreign-currency accounts NRIs can hold at Indian banks), $1.97bn via Overseas Foreign Currency Borrowings (OFCBs), and $1.34bn via External Commercial Borrowings (ECBs, i.e. foreign loans to Indian companies) done under the swap facility. That pace has beaten market expectations, and analysts are now projecting total inflows of $60-70 billion by the time the window closes.
stories.why_it_matters: A weak rupee makes everything India imports — oil, electronics, fertiliser — more expensive, which feeds inflation. This scheme is the RBI's way of fighting that without just burning through its forex reserves buying rupees directly. A stronger-than-expected response means the RBI has more ammunition, and more credibility, at exactly the moment oil-driven pressure on the rupee is at its worst in months.
stories.ripple_effects: More dollar inflows means more support for the rupee and less pressure on the RBI to hike interest rates purely to defend the currency — good for stock and bond markets. It also strengthens India's forex reserves cushion, which matters for how rating agencies and foreign investors view India's financial stability during a period of high global oil prices.
stories.why_now: The scheme's first full progress update landed today, and it comes right as the Iran-Israel war has been pushing oil toward $90 a barrel and the rupee toward record lows — so the market was watching closely to see if the RBI's countermeasure was actually working.
stories.watch_next: Whether inflows keep accelerating toward that $60-70bn analyst estimate, and whether the rupee stabilizes even if oil prices stay elevated.

key_terms.definition: A foreign-currency bank account that Non-Resident Indians can open in India; money in it isn't converted to rupees, which shields it from currency swings and makes it an attractive way to bring dollars into the country.

stories.the_lesson: Central banks don't only defend a currency by buying and selling it directly — they can also design incentives that pull in foreign money on their own, which is often cheaper and more sustainable than spending down reserves.
stories.market_link: Positive for the rupee (USDINR) and RBI forex reserves; eases pressure on Indian bond yields and banks that hold FCNR(B) deposits.

stories.title: India's monsoon is stuck in two moods at once: a below-normal July overall, but extreme flooding risk in the west right now
key_points: IMD forecasts below-normal rainfall for July nationally, after a ~40% deficit in June
But Gujarat and Madhya Maharashtra are under alert for 'extremely heavy' rain July 21–22
Fishermen told to stay off the sea near Gujarat and north Maharashtra until July 24
Northwest, northeast and east-central India may still see normal-to-above-normal rain
stories.background: India's June-to-September monsoon delivers about 70% of the country's annual rainfall and is the backbone of its farm economy — a weak or erratic monsoon can hit crop yields, rural incomes, and food inflation all at once. The India Meteorological Department (IMD) issues both a broad seasonal outlook and short-range alerts for specific states; this year the two pictures have diverged sharply, with the big-picture forecast pointing to a dry July even as specific western states face sudden extreme-rain warnings.
stories.what_happened: The IMD's July forecast points to below-normal rainfall for most of the country this month, building on a roughly 40% nationwide rainfall deficit already recorded in June, alongside above-normal daytime and nighttime temperatures. At the same time, the IMD's short-range alert (issued July 21) warns of 'extremely heavy' rainfall — meaning more than 204.4mm in 24 hours — at isolated spots in Gujarat on both July 21 and 22, and in Madhya Maharashtra on July 22. Authorities have told fishermen to stay off the sea near Gujarat and north Maharashtra through July 24 as a precaution. The northwest, northeast, and parts of east-central India are forecast to buck the dry trend with normal-to-above-normal rain.
stories.why_it_matters: A weak overall monsoon threatens crop output, reservoir levels, and rural spending power — all of which feed into food prices and the broader economy. But the same season can still produce dangerous, highly localized flooding, which is its own separate risk to lives and property even in a 'dry' year overall — the two problems require completely different responses (drought contingency planning versus flood evacuation).
stories.ripple_effects: Farmers in drought-affected regions may see lower yields and reduced income, adding pressure on food inflation and the RBI's ability to manage prices. Meanwhile, Gujarat and Maharashtra face near-term flood and disruption risk — closed fishing, waterlogging, and possible crop damage from the opposite extreme, in the same season. Hydropower generation and reservoir-dependent water supply could also be affected if the shortfall persists nationally.
stories.why_now: July is the monsoon's peak month for agriculture, and this alert reflects both an accumulating seasonal rainfall deficit becoming clearer with each passing week and a specific weather system currently bringing extreme rain to the west coast.
stories.watch_next: Whether the nationwide deficit narrows in August, and whether the extreme-rainfall alert in Gujarat and Maharashtra causes flooding or damage over the next 48 hours.

key_terms.definition: The gap between the rainfall a region actually receives and its long-term seasonal average — a large deficit signals drought risk for farming and water supply.
IMD's official category for more than 204.4mm of rain in 24 hours — a threshold associated with flash flooding and infrastructure damage.

stories.the_lesson: A single season's rainfall total can hide wildly different risks in different places — 'below normal' on average doesn't mean safe everywhere, since the same weak monsoon can still deliver dangerous localized flooding.
stories.market_link: A weak monsoon pressures rural consumption and food-linked stocks (FMCG, tractors, fertilizer); persistent rainfall deficits can also push food inflation higher, a factor the RBI weighs in setting interest rates.

stories.title: India's top refiners halt Iraqi oil shipments as the Strait of Hormuz becomes too dangerous to sail
key_points: Indian Oil Corp and Mangalore Refinery (MRPL) both pause loadings of Iraqi crude
Indian Oil scrapped a planned 2-million-barrel tanker shipment through Hormuz
India's shipping ministry has told crewing agencies not to deploy Indian sailors on ships transiting Hormuz
Iraq is one of India's largest oil suppliers under long-term contracts
stories.background: India imports over 85% of the crude oil it needs, and Iraq has long been one of its top two suppliers alongside Russia. Under India's contracts with Iraq, Indian refiners — not Iraq — are responsible for arranging the tankers that carry the oil home, which means Indian companies bear the risk if that shipping route becomes dangerous. The US-Iran war, now in its second week of intensifying strikes, runs directly through the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway at the mouth of the Persian Gulf that roughly a fifth of the world's oil passes through, and Yemen's Houthi rebels have separately declared a blockade on Saudi oil shipments through the Red Sea.
stories.what_happened: Indian Oil Corporation and Mangalore Refinery & Petrochemicals (MRPL) have both suspended fresh crude loadings from Iraq, judging that sending fully-laden tankers through the Strait of Hormuz is no longer worth the risk after a string of attacks on commercial vessels in the area. Indian Oil specifically abandoned plans to load a 2-million-barrel tanker, the Lila Jamnagar. India's shipping ministry has separately instructed shipowners and crewing agencies not to deploy Indian seafarers on any vessel transiting Hormuz until further notice — a precaution given how many merchant sailors globally are Indian nationals.
stories.why_it_matters: India is the world's third-largest oil importer, and Iraq typically supplies a large share of that under contracts that leave Indian companies exposed to shipping risk. Halting those loadings forces refiners to scramble for substitute crude from elsewhere (likely the US, West Africa, or non-Hormuz Gulf sources), often at a higher price, which raises input costs across India's fuel supply chain right as the rupee is already under pressure.
stories.ripple_effects: Higher crude-sourcing costs for Indian Oil and MRPL squeeze refining margins and can eventually show up in pump prices for petrol and diesel. The pause also adds to strain on the rupee, since India pays for oil in US dollars and any supply disruption or price spike widens the trade gap. It's a preview of what a fuller Hormuz shutdown would mean for India specifically, given how central Gulf oil is to its energy mix.
stories.why_now: Attacks on commercial tankers near Hormuz have escalated as the US-Iran war enters its second week, on top of the new Houthi blockade threat on the Red Sea side — making two of the world's key oil chokepoints simultaneously risky at once.
stories.watch_next: Whether Indian refiners can secure alternative crude supplies fast enough to avoid a squeeze on fuel margins, and whether global oil prices (already pushing toward $90/barrel) climb further if other importers follow India's lead.

key_terms.definition: A narrow sea passage between Iran and Oman that roughly a fifth of the world's oil shipments pass through — closing or endangering it can ripple through energy prices worldwide.
A narrow shipping route that a large share of global oil trade must pass through, making it a high-value target during conflicts and a source of price volatility when threatened.

stories.the_lesson: Long-term supply contracts can quietly shift risk onto the buyer — when a country agrees to arrange its own shipping, a war thousands of miles from home can still directly hit its refiners' bottom line.
stories.market_link: Indian Oil Corp and Mangalore Refinery's near-term margins are exposed; sustained higher crude costs also pressure the rupee and could feed into inflation via fuel prices.

stories.title: Indian pharma stocks slide as Trump's steep generic-drug tariff rattles a $10bn+ export industry
key_points: Sun Pharma, Cipla, Dr Reddy's, Lupin, Aurobindo all in focus/falling
India is the US's single biggest supplier of generic medicines
Tariff-free window lasts two years, so no immediate hit to earnings
Analysts split on how much real damage this can do if reshoring lags
stories.background: Indian pharmaceutical companies are the backbone of the US generic drug supply — cheap, high-volume medicines like statins, antibiotics and blood-pressure pills. This export relationship, worth well over $10 billion a year, is one of India's most important trade ties with the US, and any US tariff threat to it is closely watched on Dalal Street.
stories.what_happened: Shares of India's biggest generic drugmakers — Sun Pharma, Cipla, Dr Reddy's Laboratories, Lupin and Aurobindo Pharma — came under pressure on Wednesday, July 22, after President Trump proposed a phased tariff of up to 200% on generic drug imports into the US, starting from a 100% rate after an initial two-year tariff-free period from August 1, 2026. Some technical analysts flagged that pharma stocks could fall as much as 30% if the tariff is implemented as described, though others called the initial reaction largely sentiment-driven rather than based on confirmed earnings damage.
stories.why_it_matters: The US is the largest and most profitable market for Indian generic exporters — a serious tariff would squeeze margins on a business that runs on thin ones already. Even though the tariff doesn't bite for two years, markets price in future risk today, and this is exactly the kind of policy that can reshape a company's US strategy years in advance.
stories.ripple_effects: Indian drugmakers may need to accelerate plans for US manufacturing plants to avoid the tariff altogether, a costly and multi-year commitment. A prolonged tariff overhang could also weigh on the broader Nifty Pharma index and investor sentiment toward India's export-driven sectors more broadly.
stories.why_now: The share-price reaction is a direct, same-day response to Trump's tariff announcement — markets are pricing in the policy risk immediately even though the tariff itself won't take effect until 2028.
stories.watch_next: Whether Indian pharma majors announce concrete US investment or plant-building plans in response, and whether the US finalizes the tariff timeline as described.

key_terms.definition: Sales of off-patent medicines to other countries — India's largest pharma companies earn much of their revenue this way, especially from the US.

stories.the_lesson: A foreign tariff announced years before it takes effect can still move stock prices today — markets discount future policy risk immediately, long before the actual cost ever shows up in a quarterly result.
stories.market_link: Sun Pharma, Cipla, Dr Reddy's, Lupin, Aurobindo Pharma and the Nifty Pharma index are directly exposed.

stories.title: Assam's flood toll climbs past 10 as over 3 lakh are hit; Kashmir's Poonch, Rajouri and Doda districts report at least 15 more dead
key_points: Assam's flood death toll has risen past 10, with more than 3 lakh people affected across 11 districts
The Brahmaputra and tributaries including the Dikhow, Desang and Dhansiri are flowing above the danger mark
Over 9,000 people evacuated by boat in Assam; thousands more sheltering in relief camps
Separately, rain-triggered incidents in Jammu & Kashmir's Poonch, Rajouri and Doda districts have killed at least 15, with several still missing
stories.background: Northeast India and Jammu & Kashmir are both in the middle of an active southwest monsoon spell. Heavy rain has been triggering floods in Assam's river districts and landslides in Kashmir's hill districts since the weekend, and the toll first reported — at least 26 dead across Kashmir, Nagaland and Assam combined — has continued to rise as more remote areas report in.
stories.what_happened: As of Tuesday, Assam's flood death toll had climbed past 10, with over 3 lakh people affected in 11 districts; rivers including the Brahmaputra are flowing above the danger mark, and authorities have evacuated thousands by boat into relief camps. Separately, rain-related incidents in Jammu & Kashmir's Poonch, Rajouri and Doda districts have killed at least 15 people, with several others still missing. India's weather office has warned that active monsoon conditions and the risk of more extremely heavy rain could persist for up to another week.
stories.why_it_matters: This is an escalating, multi-state emergency, not a one-day event — the death toll is still climbing days after the flooding began, and IMD's own forecast suggests it isn't over. That strains disaster-response resources in two separate parts of the country at once.
stories.ripple_effects: Relief-camp capacity and evacuation logistics in Assam, road and transport disruption in Kashmir's hill districts, agricultural damage in flooded districts, and continued pressure to keep the suspended pilgrimage routes closed for safety.
stories.why_now: This is the peak of the southwest monsoon, and the weather office's own forecast says the wet spell has more to run — meaning the toll is likely to keep rising before it falls.
stories.watch_next: Whether IMD's forecast of another week of extremely heavy rain in Jammu & Kashmir materializes, and whether Assam's rivers cross into more severe flood-danger categories.

key_terms.definition: The water level above which authorities officially classify a river as flooding and posing a risk to nearby areas.

stories.the_lesson: A flood death toll reported on any single day is rarely the final number — in an active monsoon system, it climbs as more remote areas report in and the rain keeps falling, so early tallies are a floor, not a final count.

thread.previously: Monday's story reported flash floods across Kashmir, Nagaland and Assam had killed at least 26 people combined, forcing three major pilgrimages to be suspended.
thread.changed: The toll has kept climbing region by region: Assam alone is now past 10 dead with over 3 lakh people affected and thousands evacuated by boat, while Jammu & Kashmir's Poonch, Rajouri and Doda districts separately report at least 15 dead with more still missing — and IMD is warning the heavy rain could continue for another week.

stories.title: Supreme Court overturns part of a Kerala High Court order that had put the state Waqf Board under a government officer's supervision
key_points: Supreme Court deletes the part of a Kerala HC order placing the Waqf Board under a state Joint Secretary's supervision
The HC's July 15 order had also frozen the Board from taking major decisions or capital spending
Underlying dispute: PILs claim the Board is functioning illegally without two non-Muslim members a new national law requires
SC has asked the Kerala HC to decide that core legality question on an expedited basis
stories.background: A waqf is property permanently donated for a religious or charitable purpose under Islamic law, and a Waqf Board is the state body that manages such properties. A national law passed in 2025 reworked Waqf governance, including a requirement that state Boards include non-Muslim members — a change that has been a flashpoint nationwide between the government and Muslim community groups over religious-institution autonomy.
stories.what_happened: The Kerala Waqf Board approached the Supreme Court against a July 15 Kerala High Court interim order — issued while hearing PILs alleging the Board lacked the mandated non-Muslim members — that had frozen the Board's decision-making and placed a state Joint Secretary in supervisory charge. On July 21, the Supreme Court deleted the supervision direction, allowing the Joint Secretary to remain a Board member but not run it, while sending the core legality question back to the Kerala High Court for a fast-tracked decision.
stories.why_it_matters: This restores the Waqf Board's day-to-day independence for now, while the deeper fight over the new Waqf law's composition rules continues to play out. It's also a signal of how courts nationwide may handle the wave of similar challenges filed under the same law.
stories.ripple_effects: Similar disputes over government supervision of Waqf Boards under the new law are unfolding in other states; how the Kerala case resolves could become a reference point for those. Muslim community organisations are watching closely given how politically charged the underlying law already is.
stories.why_now: The Supreme Court took up the Waqf Board's emergency appeal because the July 15 supervision order was already in effect and blocking the Board's routine functioning.
stories.watch_next: The Kerala High Court's expedited ruling on whether the Board's current composition — without two non-Muslim members — makes it illegally constituted under the new national law.

key_terms.definition: Property permanently donated for a religious or charitable purpose under Islamic law; a Waqf Board is the state body that manages such properties.
A temporary court direction that holds until a case is fully decided — meant to preserve the situation, not settle who is right.

stories.the_lesson: Courts often separate urgent relief from the underlying legal question — restoring normal operations immediately while sending the harder constitutional dispute back for a full, considered hearing.

stories.title: 46 militants lay down arms in Manipur — the first mass surrender since the conflict began
key_points: 46 cadres of the banned KCP (People's War Group) surrendered
They handed over 28 weapons including AK rifles and a rocket launcher
It's the first such surrender by this group since Manipur's violence began
None of the 46 were part of any earlier peace agreement
stories.background: Manipur, a northeastern Indian state, has been gripped by ethnic conflict since 2023, mainly between the Meitei and Kuki-Zo communities, alongside long-running separatist insurgencies. The Kangleipak Communist Party (People's War Group), or KCP-PWG, is one of several banned armed groups operating in the Imphal Valley that have fought both the Indian state and, at times, each other, over demands ranging from greater autonomy to full independence for the Meitei homeland.
stories.what_happened: On July 21, 46 active KCP-PWG cadres surrendered to Manipur Chief Minister Y. Khemchand Singh at the Assam Rifles' Mantripukhri Garrison in Imphal East district. They voluntarily handed over 28 weapons — including AK rifles, INSAS rifles, pistols, carbines and a rocket-propelled grenade launcher — along with ammunition and explosives. Officials say the surrender is the result of weeks of outreach by the Assam Rifles, Manipur Police and state government.
stories.why_it_matters: This is described as the first mass surrender by this particular outfit since Manipur's conflict began, and notably none of the 46 had been part of any prior peace deal — meaning this is a genuinely new group choosing to disengage, not a repeat of an existing arrangement. Every cadre and weapon off the streets reduces the risk of further violence in a state still recovering from years of ethnic clashes.
stories.ripple_effects: A successful surrender can create momentum for other splinter groups to follow, especially if the state delivers on rehabilitation promises (housing, jobs, amnesty) for those who've laid down arms. It also gives the state and central government a rare good-news story on Manipur to point to, potentially easing some political pressure over the broader, unresolved ethnic conflict.
stories.why_now: The surrender is the payoff of a sustained, weeks-long persuasion effort by security forces rather than a reaction to a single trigger event — timed to a formal handover ceremony with the Chief Minister.
stories.watch_next: Watch whether the state follows through with rehabilitation packages for the surrendered cadres, and whether other Meitei or Kuki armed groups follow with surrenders of their own.

key_terms.definition: A trained, active member of an organized militant or political group — here, a fighter belonging to the KCP-PWG.

stories.the_lesson: Ending armed conflict is rarely a single peace deal — it's often a slow accumulation of smaller surrenders and local deals, each one making the next slightly more likely as fighters see that laying down arms doesn't mean losing everything.

stories.title: Union ministers visit Sonam Wangchuk in ICU as his hunger strike enters its 24th day

thread.previously: The Delhi High Court had just ordered Wangchuk moved from government-run Safdarjung Hospital to the private Medanta Hospital in Gurugram for closer monitoring, with doctors reporting pancytopenia (a drop in red cells, white cells and platelets) but stable vitals.
thread.changed: Union ministers JP Nadda and Jitendra Singh visited Wangchuk in Medanta's ICU-8 to personally enquire about his health — a direct government-level engagement that goes beyond court-ordered medical care. Wangchuk, now on day 24 of his fast, wrote a handwritten note saying he will keep fasting until protesting student leaders are allowed to meet Members of Parliament at Parliament House — a new, specific demand.

key_points: Wangchuk is on day 24 of his hunger strike, now in Medanta's ICU
BJP leaders JP Nadda and Jitendra Singh visited him in hospital
He says he'll keep fasting until student leaders can meet MPs
Move follows a Delhi High Court order shifting him from Safdarjung
stories.background: Sonam Wangchuk is a Ladakh-based education reformer and climate activist, well known for pushing India's government on Himalayan glacier protection and for inspiring the 'rocket boys' school-reform movement. He began an indefinite hunger strike on June 28 over the NEET-UG (India's national medical entrance exam) paper-leak scandal, becoming the emotional centerpiece of the wider 'Cockroach' youth protest movement demanding Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation.
stories.what_happened: Wangchuk was shifted from Delhi's government-run Safdarjung Hospital to the private Medanta Hospital in Gurugram on a Delhi High Court order, arriving in the ICU around 7:30 pm. Union Ministers JP Nadda and Jitendra Singh visited him there to ask after his health. In a handwritten note, Wangchuk said he would continue his fast until protesting student leaders are permitted to meet MPs at Parliament House — sharpening his demand beyond just the minister's resignation.
stories.why_it_matters: A senior government minister visiting a hunger-striking activist in the ICU signals that the Modi government is worried about the political and human cost of letting Wangchuk's condition deteriorate further, even as it hasn't met his core demands. It also shows the protest movement is starting to get direct engagement from the ruling party, not just the opposition.
stories.ripple_effects: If Wangchuk's health worsens further, pressure will grow on the government to make some concession — even a symbolic one like arranging the parliament meeting he's asking for — to defuse the standoff. His case keeps the broader NEET/Cockroach protest story in the headlines and gives the opposition (Congress, Samajwadi Party) a rallying point in the ongoing Monsoon Session of Parliament.
stories.why_now: His hunger strike has now stretched past three weeks and doctors have repeatedly flagged worsening blood counts, forcing the courts and now senior ministers to get directly involved rather than let the situation play out unattended.
stories.watch_next: Watch for whether the government arranges the student-leader meeting with MPs Wangchuk is demanding, and for the next court hearing or medical bulletin on his condition.

key_terms.definition: A drop in all three major blood cell types — red cells, white cells and platelets — which can happen during prolonged fasting and raises the risk of infection, bleeding and organ strain.

stories.the_lesson: When a protest movement produces one visible, personal symbol — a person literally risking their life — governments often respond to that individual case even while resisting the movement's broader demands, because managing one person's health crisis is more politically containable than conceding the underlying issue.

prev_ref.title: Sonam Wangchuk shifted to a private Gurugram hospital after Delhi High Court order, as his NEET hunger strike enters its fourth week

stories.title: The rupee nears a record low as the Iran war pushes oil higher — and traders can't tell how hard the RBI will fight back
key_points: Rupee down nearly 2% in July, hovering near an all-time low around 96-97 per dollar
RBI selling dollars in both onshore and offshore markets to slow the fall
Crude oil's climb toward $90/barrel is the main driver — India imports most of its oil
Traders say RBI's intervention looks inconsistent, creating market confusion
stories.background: Yesterday's story covered India's stock market falling for a second day as the US-Iran war pushed oil prices up. A weaker rupee and costlier oil form a 'double whammy' for India, since the country imports roughly 85% of the crude it uses and pays for it in dollars.
stories.what_happened: India's rupee has fallen almost 2% this month, the worst showing among major Asian currencies, and has repeatedly approached record-low levels near 96-97 per dollar as the US-Iran war drives crude prices higher. The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) has stepped in, selling dollars in both onshore and offshore markets to slow the currency's fall. Traders say the RBI's intervention has looked inconsistent, leaving them unable to tell what exchange-rate level the central bank is actually willing to defend.
stories.why_it_matters: A sliding currency makes all of India's dollar-priced imports — oil above all — more expensive, feeding directly into inflation and squeezing companies and consumers even if the stock market itself recovers.
stories.ripple_effects: A weaker rupee widens India's current-account deficit (the gap between what the country pays for imports versus what it earns from exports) — a sustained $10/barrel rise in oil could widen that gap by roughly 0.4-0.5% of GDP. It also draws down the RBI's foreign exchange reserves, the war chest it uses to defend the currency, and raises costs for Indian companies and travellers converting rupees into dollars.
stories.why_now: Crude prices have kept climbing as the US-Iran war entered an 11th straight night of strikes with no sign yet of a ceasefire, keeping fresh pressure on the currency day after day.
stories.watch_next: Whether the RBI clarifies, or is forced to reveal through its actions, a real floor for the rupee, and whether crude oil breaks decisively above $90 a barrel.

key_terms.definition: The gap between the money a country pays for imports (like oil) and what it earns from exports and other foreign income — a wider gap means the country needs more foreign currency than it's bringing in.

stories.the_lesson: Currency intervention only works if markets believe the central bank means it — a defense that looks inconsistent can spook traders more than no intervention at all, because nobody knows where the real line is.
stories.market_link: Oil marketing companies, airlines, and import-heavy sectors face higher costs; IT and pharma exporters that earn in dollars get a partial offset from the weaker rupee.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported the Sensex fell 238 points and the Nifty fell about 51 points in a second straight losing session, as banking and IT stocks dragged the market down on fears of oil prices pushing toward $90 a barrel and foreign investors pulling money out.
thread.changed: Today the pressure has moved from stocks to the currency itself — the rupee is nearing record lows near 96-97/dollar, and the RBI is visibly intervening by selling dollars, though traders say its approach looks inconsistent.

prev_ref.title: Sensex and Nifty fall for a second straight day as the US-Iran war pushes crude oil toward $90 a barrel

stories.title: As a protester fights for her life, the Cockroach party sacks its own spokesperson over a burger video mocking critics
key_points: CJP (Cockroach Janta Party) fires spokesperson Vijeta Dahiya
Videos showed him skipping the crackdown, then eating a burger and biscuits, mocking critics
Comes as a young woman protester remains critical on a ventilator at RML Hospital
BJP national chief J.P. Nadda visits the injured at the same hospital
stories.background: The CJP-led 'Chalo Sansad' march by NEET aspirants and students demanding accountability over the exam paper leak turned violent on Monday when police used tear gas and lathi charges, injuring well over a hundred people on both sides. One student, in her early 20s, remains on ventilator support in the ICU.
stories.what_happened: Videos surfaced showing CJP spokesperson Vijeta Dahiya absent from the march during the police crackdown, then mocking critics online by eating a burger and biscuits and joking about snacks available at the protest site. The party publicly condemned the videos as "deeply insensitive" and removed him from his post. Separately, senior BJP leader J.P. Nadda visited RML Hospital to check on those injured, including police personnel, as the female student's critical condition continued.
stories.why_it_matters: A protest movement built on moral authority — young people demanding accountability while one of their own fights for her life — is now managing its own internal discipline problem, at the exact moment its opponents in government are making a visible show of concern for the injured.
stories.ripple_effects: The spokesperson episode risks becoming a distraction rival parties and critics can use to question the movement's seriousness, even as the underlying grievance — the NEET paper leak and the crackdown — continues to dominate Parliament's logjammed monsoon session. Political outreach like Nadda's hospital visit also signals the government is working to manage the story's optics.
stories.why_now: The videos of Dahiya went viral the same day the injured student's condition became a national story, sharpening the contrast between a leader seen mocking the moment and a young woman fighting for her life because of it.
stories.watch_next: The student's medical condition, and whether the spokesperson controversy affects the CJP's ability to keep the wider coalition of opposition parties and student groups united behind it.
stories.the_lesson: A protest movement's credibility rests on its members living up to the sacrifice its most visible victims are making — even one leader caught behaving badly can hand critics a story bigger than the cause itself.

thread.previously: Yesterday we reported the protest entered its second day at Jantar Mantar, with roughly 60 protesters and 118 police injured in Monday's clashes, a young woman critical on a ventilator at RML Hospital, and Congress leaders including Rahul and Priyanka Gandhi detained after marching on PM Modi's residence demanding the education minister's resignation.
thread.changed: Today the movement is managing an internal scandal — sacking its own spokesperson over an insensitive viral video — even as the injured student remains critical and BJP's national chief makes a hospital visit to the injured.

prev_ref.title: A day after Delhi's crackdown, India's youth-led 'Cockroach' protest regroups — Congress demands Modi and his education minister resign

stories.title: Sikkim tunnel death toll climbs to 20 as gas and flooding hamper the hunt for 5 still trapped
key_points: Death toll up from 7 to 20 as recovery work continues
5 workers still trapped; rescuers battling gas and water inside
Some rescue personnel felt dizzy or lost consciousness from gas
Centre and Sikkim government announce compensation for families
stories.background: A methane gas explosion tore through an under-construction tunnel at NHPC's Teesta Stage-VI hydropower project in Sikkim's Namchi district, triggering a collapse that trapped workers roughly 1.5 km inside. NHPC is India's state-owned hydropower company; tunnelling in the Himalayas is especially risky because the rock is unstable and pockets of natural gas can build up underground, as this project has shown.
stories.what_happened: Rescue teams have now recovered 20 bodies, up from the initial count of 7, with 5 workers still unaccounted for more than a day after the collapse. The operation has been slowed by leftover gas inside the tunnel — some rescuers have felt dizzy or briefly lost consciousness while trying to reach trapped workers — and by heavy monsoon rain sending water pooling into the tunnel. Sikkim's Chief Minister Prem Singh Tamang announced ₹4 lakh (about $4,800) in compensation for each deceased worker's family and ₹50,000 for the injured; the Prime Minister separately announced a further ₹2 lakh per family from the central government.
stories.why_it_matters: The rising toll makes this one of the deadliest workplace disasters at an Indian infrastructure site in recent years, and it sharpens questions about whether NHPC and its contractors had adequate gas-detection and evacuation systems in place before workers were sent 1.5 km into the mountain.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect a formal safety investigation, likely scrutiny of other Himalayan tunnelling projects for similar gas risks, and pressure on NHPC over contractor oversight. The human toll — now three times the initial count — will keep this in the national conversation and could slow momentum on hydropower projects across the region until safety reviews are complete.
stories.why_now: Rescue crews are still working through debris and gas pockets more than a day on, and the death toll is rising as recovery — not just rescue — becomes the main task; monsoon rain is actively working against them.
stories.watch_next: Whether the final 5 missing workers are found alive or the toll rises further, and whether the government orders tunnelling paused across other Himalayan hydro sites pending a safety audit.

key_terms.definition: A colourless, highly flammable natural gas that can seep into tunnels and mines and explode if it builds up and meets a spark.

stories.the_lesson: In hazardous infrastructure work, the true cost of a disaster often only becomes clear well after the initial headline — early death tolls in confined-space accidents are frequently undercounts, because recovery in dangerous conditions is slower and harder than the rescue effort itself.
stories.market_link: NHPC shares face continued pressure on project-delay and reputational risk as the toll rises; contractors and equipment suppliers tied to Himalayan hydro projects may also see scrutiny.

thread.previously: A methane explosion collapsed the NHPC Teesta Stage-VI tunnel in Namchi district, killing at least 7 with several workers still trapped; the PM and Home Minister had spoken with Sikkim's Chief Minister as rescue efforts began.
thread.changed: The confirmed death toll has nearly tripled to 20, with 5 workers still trapped; rescue crews are now battling residual gas and monsoon flooding inside the tunnel, and both the state and central governments have announced compensation for victims' families.

prev_ref.title: A methane blast collapses a hydro-project tunnel in Sikkim — at least 7 killed, many feared trapped

stories.title: BCCI overhauls domestic rules: match-long bans for deliberate no-balls, new overthrow and wide-ball tweaks
key_points: Bowlers caught deliberately bowling no-balls or full tosses now face suspension for the rest of the match, not just the innings
Overthrows are redefined to only count when a fielder genuinely tries to run a batter out
A new marking helps umpires judge leg-side wides, and keepers no longer must stay behind the stumps during the bowler's run-up
The changes kick in from 1 August, just ahead of the Duleep Trophy
stories.background: The BCCI (Board of Control for Cricket in India) runs India's domestic cricket pyramid — tournaments like the Ranji and Duleep Trophies that develop the players who go on to play for India. 'Playing conditions' are rule tweaks a board layers on top of cricket's core Laws, which are written globally by the MCC (Marylebone Cricket Club). The BCCI's changes arrive about two months ahead of the MCC's own global law updates, which take effect worldwide on 1 October.
stories.what_happened: The BCCI has circulated new domestic playing conditions to all state cricket associations. The headline change: a bowler found guilty of deliberately bowling front-foot no-balls or full-toss ('non-landing') deliveries — tactics sometimes used to disrupt a batter's rhythm — will now be suspended for the rest of the match, up from just the rest of that innings. Overthrows are redefined to count only when a fielder's throw was a genuine attempt to break the stumps or effect a run-out, closing a grey area with simple misfields. Umpires get a new crease marking to help judge leg-side wides, and wicketkeepers no longer have to keep their gloves behind the stumps for the entire bowler's run-up, only from the moment of delivery. A separate change requires the last scheduled over of a day's play in multi-day matches to be bowled in full even if a wicket falls mid-over.
stories.why_it_matters: These rules shape how India's next generation of cricketers plays and gets officiated, closing two genuinely contentious loopholes — deliberately bowling a no-ball to unsettle an in-form batter, and disputed overthrow calls — with real deterrents rather than just guidelines.
stories.ripple_effects: Because the BCCI's domestic system feeds India's national team, habits corrected here tend to surface later at the international level; India's domestic season effectively becomes an early trial run of rules the rest of the cricket world adopts globally two months later.
stories.why_now: The changes take effect from 1 August, timed to start with India's new domestic season, which opens with the Duleep Trophy on 23 August.
stories.watch_next: How umpires apply the new deliberate-no-ball rule once the Duleep Trophy begins, and whether any bowler actually gets suspended for a full match under it — a real test case would be the first sign of how strictly it's enforced.

key_terms.definition: The historic body that writes cricket's official Laws globally, separate from any individual country's board.
Extra runs the batting side gets when a fielder's return throw misses the stumps or wicketkeeper and runs away.
An illegal delivery — for example, the bowler overstepping the crease — that gives the batting side a free run and usually an extra 'free hit' ball.

stories.the_lesson: Rules in any competitive system tend to get rewritten reactively, closing specific loopholes only after players have already found and exploited them — watching which loopholes get closed tells you what's actually been happening on the field, not just what the rulebook says should happen.

stories.title: West Indies deny New Zealand a clean sweep, win the fifth ODI as series ends 3-2
key_points: West Indies chased down 269 to win the fifth and final ODI by 2 wickets, with 15 balls to spare
Shimron Hetmyer's unbeaten 69 anchored the chase and won him Player of the Match
New Zealand had already sealed the series 3-1 before this dead-rubber match
Final result: New Zealand win the series 3-2, missing out on a whitewash
stories.background: This was the fifth and final match of a five-game One Day International (ODI) series between touring New Zealand and hosts West Indies, played across Guyana and Barbados through July. New Zealand had already wrapped up the series with a nail-biting one-wicket win in the fourth match, making this last game a 'dead rubber' — one that no longer decided the series, but still mattered for pride, ranking points and individual form.
stories.what_happened: New Zealand batted first at Kensington Oval in Bridgetown and posted 268/9 in 50 overs, built on half-centuries from Tom Latham (69), Nick Kelly (64) and Will Young (56). West Indies slipped to 123/4 chasing 269, but Shimron Hetmyer built a 91-run partnership with Sherfane Rutherford before finishing the chase unbeaten on 69, getting his side home in 47.3 overs — a two-wicket win with 15 balls left. Hetmyer was named Player of the Match.
stories.why_it_matters: For West Indies, salvaging a win in a match that no longer decided anything still matters — it's ranking points, a morale boost, and a clear form marker for Hetmyer heading into his next assignments. For New Zealand, the series win is banked, but losing the finale while defending a total will get scrutinized.
stories.ripple_effects: The result feeds into both teams' ODI points tally, relevant to World Cup qualification pathways, and gives Hetmyer momentum heading into his next cricket, whether franchise or international.
stories.why_now: The match was played and completed on 21 July, the final fixture of New Zealand's tour of the West Indies.
stories.watch_next: Whether Hetmyer's form carries into his next series, and how each board's year-end review frames the tour.

key_terms.definition: One Day International — a format where each team bats for a maximum of 50 overs, one level below Test cricket in prestige.
A match played after a series has already been decided, so the result no longer changes who wins the series.
The runs two batters add together while both are still batting, before either gets out.

stories.the_lesson: A result stops mattering for the scoreboard the moment a series is decided, but it never stops mattering for the players — individual form, confidence and ranking points keep accumulating long after the headline outcome is settled.

stories.title: New Zealand eye a series sweep as West Indies fight for pride in the ODI decider
key_points: New Zealand have already sealed the 5-match series 3-1 after West Indies collapsed for 138, 140 and 188 in three straight defeats
Left-arm spinner Jayden Lennox has been the standout, taking 11 wickets in 4 matches in a series where spinners have taken a West-Indies-record 42 wickets
West Indies' Keacy Carty (174 runs) is the lone batting bright spot as the hosts try to avoid a full series sweep at Kensington Oval, Bridgetown
It's a dead rubber for the series outcome, but a chance for West Indies to salvage pride before hosting Pakistan for a marquee Test series
stories.background: New Zealand toured the West Indies for a five-match ODI series. West Indies won the opener in Providence, but New Zealand hit back with three straight wins, clinching the series 3-1 with one match to go, exposing a West Indies batting order that has repeatedly struggled against spin.
stories.what_happened: The two sides play the fifth and final ODI of the series today at Kensington Oval in Bridgetown, Barbados — a dead rubber with the series already decided, but with West Indies looking to avoid a complete sweep after being bowled out cheaply in three consecutive matches.
stories.why_it_matters: The result won't change the series outcome, but it's a significant test of whether West Indies' young batting order can find some form and confidence against spin bowling before a big home assignment against Pakistan.
stories.ripple_effects: A heavy defeat would deepen concerns about West Indies' white-ball batting depth heading into a busy home season; a win would offer a small confidence boost before the Pakistan Tests.
stories.why_now: It's simply the final match of the series, played today.
stories.watch_next: Whether West Indies avoid a 4-1 series loss, Jayden Lennox's spin performance, and the top order's form heading into the Pakistan Test series.
stories.the_lesson: A team's repeated collapse against one bowling type — here, spin — in a single series usually exposes a broader technical gap that opponents will keep targeting until it's fixed.

stories.title: Pakistan drop senior pacers Shaheen Afridi, Naseem Shah and Hasan Ali for the West Indies Tests
key_points: Shaheen Shah Afridi, Naseem Shah, Hasan Ali and spinner Noman Ali all left out
Selectors say it's about current red-ball workload and form, not a loss of faith
